Forged in Identity

Samurai Weapons and the Haitorei Edict of 1876

刀 弓 誇

For centuries, the samurai class defined itself through its weapons. The sword was not merely a weapon, it was an extension of themselves, an extension of their spirit. When the Meiji government issued the Haitorei Edict on March 28, 1876, it devastated the very core of the Samurai identity. The edict was part of a broader campaign to centralize power and demolish the feudal class that had ruled Japan under the Tokugawa Shogunate.


This digital exhibit presents five artifacts from the collection of the author. Each object tells a story not just of craftsmanship and function, but of a society in transformation. Examined together, they reveal how deeply integrated the Samurai's tools were with their sense of self, and how the loss of these objects represented something far more destructive than just disarmament, the purposeful attempt at erasing of their identity.


The Charter Oath of 1868 had already signaled that “evil customs of the past shall be broken off” and that Japan would open itself to the world and its knowledge. Thinkers like Fukuzawa Yukichi argued in his writings such as An Encouragement of Learning (1872) that practical education instead of hereditary rights should determine a person's worth. By the time the Haitorei was issued, the samurai class found itself defined by the swords they could no longer carry. Inazo Nitobe would later argue in Bushido: The Soul of Japan (1900) that the samurai code could survive the abolition of the class itself with the objects in this exhibition representing the endurance of these objects and their spirit.

Artifact I

The Daishō

大小 — "Big and Small"

A daishō displayed on a katanakake (sword stand). The wakizashi sits above in its koshirae (battle-ready fittings), and the katana rests below in its shirasaya (storage mount). From the author's personal collection.

A second daishō pair, both swords dressed in their koshirae. Note the distinct wrapping styles and lacquer work on each saya.

A katana in koshirae with black-lacquered saya and blue silk tsuka-ito wrapping.

Object Type
Paired swords (katana & wakizashi)
Origin
Japan
Materials
Tamahagane steel, wood, ray skin, silk cord, lacquer
Shown
Multiple examples from the author's collection

What Is It?

The daishō is a pair of swords typically carried together by samurai. It’s made up of a katana (typically over 60 centimeters in blade length) and a wakizashi (usually between 30 and 60 centimeters). The word just means “big and small” referring to the two blades differing length. During the Edo period, only samurai were legally allowed to wear the daishō in public, making them a visible marker of status and class privilege. Carrying these weapons was not only practical, but served as a social hierarchical distinction between samurai and others in public.

Who Made It and How?

Japanese swordsmiths crafted these blades in a lengthy, and highly skilled process. It started with smelting iron sand (due to Japan not having reliable iron ore) in a clay furnace called a tatara to produce tamahagane, a legendary carbon steel. The smith would then repeatedly fold and hammer the steel to remove impurities. Then comes the hardening process where clay is applied to the blade before quenching. This process is what produces both the curved shape and the hamon (temper line). The hamon line is often laid in a manner to appear as clouds or streaks of lightning across the blade. The craft was considered sacred, and smiths would perform Shinto purification rituals before starting their work.

How Was It Used?

In battle, the katana was the primary weapon for close combat. It was wielded with two hands using refined combat techniques. The wakizashi served as a sidearm for close-quarters fighting, and in serious conditions, would serve as the instrument of seppuku (ritualistic suicide). During the long peace of the Edo period, the daishō's main purpose shifted from being a weapon to being a status symbol. Samurai wore their swords daily as proof of their rank, even though actual combat became rare. This daily wearing of the daishō is exactly what the Haitorei of 1876 targeted. The Meiji government's proclamation stripped samurai of the right to carry their swords, severing this important link to their identity and social status.1

What Does It Reveal?

The daishō shows how identity in Tokugawa era Japan was something you physically wore. Social hierarchy wasn’t just a bureaucratic concept, it dominated all facets of social interaction. A man wearing two swords was instantly recognizable as samurai. The Charter Oath of 1868 had declared that “all classes shall unite” in carrying out the nation’s affairs, and Fukuzawa Yukichi’s An Encouragement of Learning (1872) argued that education and merit, not inherited privilege, should determine a person’s standing.2, 3 The daishō, as an inherited privilege made of steel, went directly against these modernizing ideas. Banning it through the Haitorei was both practical (disarming a potential threat to the new government) and deeply symbolic (erasing the most visible sign of the old feudal order). The anger this caused exploded in the Satsuma Rebellion of 1877, which the American teacher John Capen Hubbard documented in his letters.5

Home The Ya (Arrow)

Artifact II

The Ya

矢 — Samurai Arrow

A Japanese war arrow (ya) displayed at full length, showing the bamboo shaft, feather fletching, and forged steel arrowhead. From the author's personal collection.

Detail of the arrowhead (yajiri), showing the pierced floral mon (clan crest) cut into the steel and the smith's signature engraved on the face. Just like swordsmiths signed their blades, arrow-makers marked their work with their name and lineage.

Object Type
War arrow (ya)
Origin
Japan
Materials
Bamboo shaft, forged steel point, feather fletchings
Notable Detail
Arrowhead bears smith's signature and pierced clan crest

What Is It?

The ya is a Japanese arrow meant to be fired from a Japanese longbow named a yumi. Japanese arrows were longer than Western arrows, often over 90 centimeters, to match the yumi’s longer draw length. The arrowhead, or yajiri, was forged from steel and came in different shapes and designs depending on its purpose. There were broad, leaf-shaped heads for cutting, narrow armor-piercing points, and even bulbous “whistling” heads (kabura-ya) used to alert others or signal the start of battle. This particular example has a decorative pierced arrowhead with a chrysanthemum mon (clan crest), which suggests it was likely a ceremonial arrowhead or belonged to a high-ranking warrior.

Who Made It and How?

Arrow making was a specialized work done by artisans known as ya-shi. The arrowhead was forged by a metalsmith with intricate details, and as shown, they would often sign their work. The shaft was shaped from bamboo, carefully selected for straightness and dried over months. Feathers from hawks, eagles, or cranes were commonly used with specific feathers used to signal the rank of the warrior. The process was highly precise. An improperly balanced arrow would fly unpredictably which could result in fatal errors.

How Was It Used?

Archery (kyūjutsu) was one of the foundational martial arts of the samurai, serving as the primary weapon of the samurai. In the early medieval period, mounted archery was the main form of combat, and a samurai's skill with the bow was considered the ultimate measure of his ability. Formal mounted archery (yabusame) continued as an important ceremony throughout the Edo period, even as firearms gradually replaced the bow on the battlefield. The arrow represents an older layer of samurai identity, one that connects the warrior class to its origins as mounted zoning fighters, long before the sword became known as their main identity bearing weapon.

What Does It Reveal?

The ya shows that samurai identity wasn't static, but rather evolved over centuries. While the Haitorei specifically targeted sword-carrying, the broader Meiji reforms dismantled the entire martial framework that had given objects like the arrow their cultural meaning. Nitobe Inazō, writing in Bushido: The Soul of Japan (1900), emphasized that the samurai code covered far more than swordsmanship. It included archery, horsemanship, and a full range of martial disciplines that together formed the warrior's sense of self.4 The signed arrowhead in this exhibit is a good example of this. It shows that even arrow-making carried the same culture of individual craftsmanship and pride that defined swordsmithing. The arrow reminds us that what the Meiji era dismantled wasn't just the right to carry a sword, but an entire culture of martial practice built over centuries.

The Daishō The Koshirae

Artifact III

The Koshirae

拵え — Sword Mountings

A tantō (short blade) shown in two states. Above, it's dressed in its koshirae with red-lacquered saya and leather-wrapped tsuka. Below, the same blade rests in its shirasaya for storage. From the author's personal collection.

The same tantō pair unsheathed, revealing the blades alongside their respective mountings.

A kozuka, the small utility knife that slides into a pocket on the sword's saya. The handle features gold and shakudō inlay depicting a tiger and dragon. The smith's signature is visible on the blade.

Object Type
Sword mountings (koshirae) and accessories (kozuka)
Origin
Japan
Materials
Lacquered wood, ray skin (same), silk/leather cord, copper/iron alloy fittings, gold inlay
Accessories Shown
Kozuka (utility knife) with tiger-and-dragon shakudō handle

What Is It?

The koshirae is the full set of external mountings and fittings that dress a Japanese sword. It includes the scabbard (saya), the handle (tsuka) wrapped in ray skin and cord, the handguard (tsuba), and smaller fittings like the menuki (decorative grip ornaments), fuchi and kashira (collar and pommel caps), and the habaki (blade collar). While the blade was considered the soul of the sword, the koshirae was its public face, the part visible to everyone when the samurai wore it at his side. The koshirae also held accessories like the kozuka, a small utility knife that slid into a slot on the saya, serving as a practical everyday tool while also showing off the metalworker's art.

Who Made It and How?

The koshirae was the work of multiple specialized artisans working together. The sayashi (scabbard maker) carved and lacquered the saya from magnolia wood, chosen for being lightweight and resistant to moisture. The tsukamaki-shi handled wrapping, applying the ray skin base first and then wrapping the handle in silk, cotton, or leather cord using traditional patterns. Metalworkers crafted the tsuba and other fittings, often decorating them with nature scenes, mythological creatures, or clan insignia using techniques like inlay, carving, and patination. The kozuka shown here is a good example of this. Its handle features a gold tiger and shakudō dragon in high relief, showing off the metalworker's skill with multiple alloys. A single set of quality koshirae could involve five or more craftsmen and take months to complete.

How Was It Used?

The koshirae turned a bare blade into a wearable, functional weapon. It protected the blade during carry, gave a secure grip during use, and communicated the owner's taste, wealth, and social standing. During the Edo period, elaborate koshirae became a way for samurai to express individuality within the rigid Tokugawa class system. The design of a samurai's sword fittings could signal his domain, his rank, his aesthetic taste, and even his political loyalties. The koshirae worked like clothing or jewelry, a form of social communication worn on the body.

What Does It Reveal?

The koshirae shows that the samurai sword wasn't just a weapon, but a way of expressing personal and social identity. As Eiko Ikegami argues in The Taming of the Samurai, samurai honor was shaped by the tension between collectivist duties and individualist desires. The koshirae captures this tension well. Its form followed strict traditional conventions, yet within those conventions, a samurai could still show personal taste. When the Charter Oath declared in 1868 that Japan would break from “evil customs of the past,” it started a process that would make this entire craft tradition obsolete.3 The Haitorei didn't just ban wearing swords, it eliminated the social context in which koshirae had meaning. Without samurai to wear them, these works of art became relics instead of expressions of living identity.

The Ya The Hamon

Artifact IV

The Hamon

刃文 — The Temper Line

An unsigned katana blade displayed without fittings, showing the full sweep of the hamon temper line along the cutting edge. From the author's personal collection.

Detail of the kissaki (point). The hamon is clearly visible as a misty, cloud-like boundary between the hardened edge and the softer spine.

Close-up of the hamon near the tip, revealing the complex, almost crystalline texture where hard martensite meets softer pearlite within the steel.

Object Type
Blade detail — hamon (temper pattern)
Origin
Japan
Materials
Tamahagane steel with differential clay tempering
Blade Shown
Unsigned katana from the author's collection

What Is It?

The hamon is the visible temper line that runs along the edge of a Japanese sword blade, appearing as a misty, cloud-like boundary between the harder cutting edge and the softer spine. It's not painted or etched onto the blade, it's a natural result of the differential hardening process and is built into the steel's crystalline structure. The hamon is one of the most studied features in Japanese sword appraisal, as its pattern is unique to each blade and can often be traced to a specific school, region, or even individual smith. Common hamon patterns include suguha (straight), midare (irregular), chōji (clove-shaped), and gunome (zigzag).

Who Made It and How?

The hamon is created during the quenching stage of blade production. Before heating the blade for its final hardening, the smith applies a mixture of clay, charcoal powder, and other materials to the blade's surface. The clay goes on thick along the spine and thin (or not at all) along the cutting edge. When the blade is heated to the right temperature, judged by the color of the glowing steel, and plunged into water, the different thicknesses of clay cause the edge to cool rapidly (forming hard martensite) while the spine cools slowly (staying as softer pearlite). The boundary between these two structures is the hamon. The pattern of the clay and the smith's control of temperature and quench speed determine how the hamon looks. This step is considered the most critical part of sword-making, often accompanied by ritual.

How Was It Used?

The hamon has two purposes: functional and aesthetic. On the functional side, the differential hardening gives the blade an extremely hard cutting edge that holds a razor-sharp polish, while the softer spine provides flexibility and shock absorption that keeps the blade from shattering on impact. This combination of hardness where you cut and flexibility where you absorb force is what made Japanese swords so effective. On the aesthetic side, the hamon became a key way of evaluating and appreciating swords. Collectors and appraisers study the hamon to identify the school and period of a blade, and a well-defined hamon significantly increases a sword's value.

What Does It Reveal?

The hamon shows where science, art, and spirituality meet in samurai material culture. A sword wasn't just forged, it was created through a process that combined metallurgical knowledge built up over centuries with aesthetic sensibility and ritual. Nitobe wrote in Bushido that the sword was the “soul of the samurai,” and the hamon is perhaps the most literal example of that idea. It's an invisible process made visible in the steel itself, unique and unrepeatable, like a fingerprint of the smith's skill.4 The fact that Japanese swords are still studied by their hamon patterns today, long after the samurai class was abolished, supports Nitobe's argument that the values in these objects outlasted the social structures that created them. The hamon stands as evidence that the samurai's material culture was never purely functional, but always an expression of identity and craft philosophy.

The Koshirae The Shirasaya

Artifact V

The Shirasaya

白鞘 — "White Scabbard"

A shirasaya bearing sayagaki, brush-written inscriptions that identify this as a Muromachi-period blade from Mino province. The kanji details the smith's attribution and the blade's measurements, effectively turning the storage mount into a portable historical record.

Detail of the shirasaya showing the mekugi-ana (pin hole) and the small bamboo mekugi pin that holds the blade in its handle. The only thing securing a Japanese sword blade to its mount is a single piece of bamboo.

A tantō shown in both its koshirae (battle dress, top) and shirasaya (storage, bottom). Two lives for one blade.

Object Type
Storage mount (shirasaya) with sayagaki inscriptions
Blade Attribution
Muromachi Period, Mino Province
Origin
Japan
Materials
Untreated honoki (magnolia) wood, bamboo pin

What Is It?

The shirasaya is a plain wooden mount used to store and protect a Japanese sword blade when it's not being worn or displayed. Unlike the koshirae, which is designed for carry and use, the shirasaya is purely utilitarian. It's a tight-fitting wooden handle and scabbard made from untreated honoki (Japanese magnolia) wood. The wood is chosen because it doesn't contain oils or resins that could damage the blade over time. The shirasaya has no guard, no wrapping, and no decorative fittings. Its name means “white scabbard,” referring to the pale color of the unfinished wood. The blade is held in place by a single bamboo pin (mekugi) passing through the handle, a surprisingly minimal solution for holding a weapon together.

Who Made It and How?

Shirasaya were made by sayashi who specialized in fitting wooden mounts precisely to individual blades. Each shirasaya is custom-made for its specific blade, with the interior cavity carved to match the blade's exact curvature, width, and thickness so the blade fits snugly without moving. The wood is left unfinished so it can breathe and absorb moisture, which protects the steel from rust. As seen in this example, important information about the blade like the smith's name, the blade's length, period, and province of origin was often written in ink (sayagaki) directly on the shirasaya's surface, turning the storage mount into a portable record of the blade's history.

How Was It Used?

During the Edo period, a samurai might own multiple koshirae sets for a single blade, swapping them for different occasions, while the shirasaya served as the blade's “home” during storage. After the Haitorei was issued in 1876 and public sword-wearing was banned, the shirasaya took on a new significance. Samurai families who chose to keep their blades rather than surrender or sell them stored their swords in shirasaya, tucked away in closets and storerooms. The shirasaya became the vessel through which samurai swords survived the Meiji era, hidden from the public view that the government had outlawed but preserved in the private spaces of families who refused to let go.1

What Does It Reveal?

The shirasaya shows the tension between public compliance and private preservation that defined how samurai responded to modernization. On the surface, families obeyed the Haitorei and swords disappeared from public life. But in their homes, blades rested in shirasaya, maintained and passed down through generations. This quiet defiance lines up with what Nitobe would later write, that the spirit of bushido could persist even when its outward symbols were suppressed.4 The shirasaya also shows something about how Japanese society handles cultural loss, not through dramatic destruction, but through careful preservation. The American observer John Capen Hubbard, writing during the Satsuma Rebellion, noted the deep emotional attachment samurai had to their weapons and way of life. The shirasaya is physical proof of that attachment, having survived quietly for over a century.5 Many of the finest swords in museums and private collections today survived specifically because they were stored in shirasaya during the decades when owning a sword was no longer a mark of status but a private family heirloom.

The Hamon Bonus: Origami

Bonus

Origami Papers

折紙 · 鑑定書 — Sword Authentication Documents

An NBTHK Hozon Tōken certificate (鑑定書) for the Muromachi-period wakizashi shown in the Shirasaya section of this exhibit. The certificate authenticates the blade as the work of Kanemitsu of Seki (Mino Province), measuring approximately 37.6 cm, and was issued on August 9, 2004. From the author's personal collection.

Object Type
NBTHK Hozon Tōken certificate (鑑定書 — Kanteisho)
Certificate No.
368451
Blade Classification
Wakizashi (脇指)
Smith Attribution
Kanemitsu (兼光作) — Seki, Mino Province (関)
Blade Length
1 shaku, 2 sun, 4 bu ≈ 37.6 cm
Grade
Hozon Tōken (保存刀剣 — "Sword Worthy of Preservation")
Date Issued
August 9, 2004 (Heisei 16)
Issuing Body
NBTHK (日本美術刀剣保存協会)

What Is It?

When it comes to Japanese swords, “origami” doesn't refer to the art of paper folding. It refers to official certificates of authenticity and appraisal, literally “folded paper,” that have accompanied important swords since the Edo period. The most well-known historical origami were issued by the Hon’ami family, who served as official sword appraisers to the Tokugawa shoguns. Today the tradition continues through the NBTHK (Nihon Bijutsu Tōken Hozon Kyōkai, the Society for the Preservation of the Japanese Art Sword), which issues graded certificates at four levels: Hozon (Worthy of Preservation), Tokubetsu Hozon (Especially Worthy of Preservation), Jūyō (Important), and Tokubetsu Jūyō (Especially Important). This certificate is a Hozon, the entry-level authentication confirming the blade as genuine and historically significant.

Reading the Certificate

The certificate identifies this blade as a wakizashi made by Kanemitsu of Seki in Mino Province, a lineage of smiths active during the Muromachi period (1336–1573). The (関) next to the smith’s name indicates that the NBTHK attributes this to the Seki Kanemitsu tradition, separate from the more famous Bizen Kanemitsu (備前長船兼光) of an earlier era. The certificate includes a photograph of the blade’s nakago (tang), which bears the smith’s signature, along with the official red seals of the NBTHK. The text reads: “The above, having been examined by this Association, is hereby certified as a Hozon Tōken.” It's a formal statement that this blade has been judged authentic and worthy of preservation by Japan’s top authority on sword authentication.

What Does It Reveal?

Origami papers show that samurai swords were never treated as disposable tools. They were appraised, cataloged, and valued as cultural treasures even when they were still in active military use. The existence of a formal appraisal system going back centuries speaks to the deep cultural significance of the sword in Japanese society. After the Haitorei, origami papers became even more important as the main way to document and preserve the history of swords no longer publicly displayed. That this blade, forged in Muromachi-era Mino, stored in a shirasaya with sayagaki, and certified by the NBTHK in 2004, can still be traced and authenticated today is a testament to how Japanese sword culture has persisted across five centuries. The origami system is one of the clearest connections between the samurai era and today. It's proof that while the Meiji government could abolish a class, it couldn't abolish the respect for the objects that class had created.

The Shirasaya Sources

Bibliography

Primary Sources

Referenced throughout this exhibit

Source 1

Meiji Government of Japan. "Haitōrei (Sword Abolishment Edict)." Proclamation No. 38 of the Daijō-kan. March 28, 1876. Japan's Samurai Revolution. Omeka digital exhibit. Accessed April 5, 2026. https://samurairevolution.omeka.net/exhibits/show/jy/section2.

The edict that formally banned public carrying of swords, stripping samurai of their most visible marker of identity.

Source 2

Fukuzawa, Yukichi. "An Encouragement of Learning." 1872. World History Commons. Accessed April 5, 2026. https://worldhistorycommons.org/encouragement-learning.

Fukuzawa's argument for universal practical education challenged the hereditary class distinctions that had given samurai their elevated status.

Source 3

Meiji, Emperor. "Gokajō no Goseimon" [Charter Oath of Five Articles]. April 6, 1868. In Sources of Japanese Tradition, edited by Wm. Theodore de Bary, Donald Keene, George Tanabe, and Paul Varley. New York: Columbia University Press, 1964. Accessed April 5, 2026. https://afe.easia.columbia.edu/ps/japan/charter_oath_1868.pdf.

The founding declaration of the Meiji government, which set the stage for reforms that would dismantle the feudal class system.

Source 4

Nitobe, Inazō. Bushido: The Soul of Japan. Philadelphia: Leeds & Biddle, 1900. Accessed April 5, 2026. https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/12096.

Written by a samurai descendant to explain the warrior code to Western audiences, arguing that bushido could survive the abolition of the class itself.

Source 5

"The Satsuma Rebellion: Letters of John Capen Hubbard." Edited by Elizabeth Tripler Nock. The Far Eastern Quarterly 7, no. 4 (1948): 373–396. Accessed April 5, 2026. https://www.jstor.org/stable/2049726.

Eyewitness account of the 1877 Satsuma Rebellion from an American teacher in Japan, documenting the samurai's violent response to the Haitorei and other Meiji reforms.

Secondary Source

Ikegami, Eiko. The Taming of the Samurai: Honorific Individualism and the Making of Modern Japan. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1997.

A sociological analysis of how samurai honor culture evolved through Japan's transition from feudalism to modernity.
